content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "\u4e00\u8d77\u4f18\u8bfa",
  "game_name": "Color Match Royale",
  "genre": "Card Game",
  "core_mechanics": "The game simulates a single-player version of UNO against multiple AI opponents. Players take turns playing cards from their hand onto a discard pile, matching the top card's color or number/symbol. The objective is to be the first to empty one's hand.\n\n**Player Actions & Triggers:**\n*   **Play Card:** Player clicks/taps on a card in their hand. If the card is legally playable (matches color, number/symbol of the discard pile's top card, or is a Wild card), it moves to the discard pile. If not, an error sound plays.\n*   **Draw Card:** Player clicks/taps on the Draw Pile. A card is drawn and added to the player's hand. This is also automatically triggered if the player tries to play a card and has no playable cards in hand.\n*   **Call 'UNO':** For higher difficulty levels (Level 4+), the player must press 'Z' when they have only one card left in their hand, immediately after playing a card that brings their hand size to one. Failure to do so before the next player's turn results in drawing 2 penalty cards.\n*   **Choose Color (Wild Cards):** After playing a Wild or Wild Draw Four card, a color selection prompt appears (Red, Green, Blue, Yellow buttons). Player clicks/taps to choose the new active color.\n\n**Rules & Systems:**\n*   **Deck:** Standard 108-card UNO deck (4 colors: Red, Green, Blue, Yellow; Numbers 0-9; Action cards: Skip, Reverse, Draw Two; Wild cards: Wild, Wild Draw Four).\n*   **Dealing:** Each player (human and AI) is dealt an initial hand of 7 cards (can vary by level).\n*   **Starting Card:** The top card of the draw pile is flipped to start the discard pile. If it's an action or wild card, special rules apply (e.g., first player draws 2 for Draw Two).\n*   **Turn Order:** Moves clockwise by default. A 'Reverse' card changes direction.\n*   **Matching:** A card can be played if it matches the current discard pile's color OR number/symbol. Wild cards can be played on any card.\n*   **Action Card Effects:**\n    *   **Skip:** Skips the next player's turn.\n    *   **Reverse:** Reverses the direction of play.\n    *   **Draw Two:** Next player draws 2 cards and loses their turn.\n    *   **Wild:** The player chooses the new active color.\n    *   **Wild Draw Four:** The player chooses the new active color. The next player draws 4 cards and loses their turn. This card can only be played if the player has no card matching the current color (this rule is enforced for the human player, but AI might 'bluff' for simplified AI).\n*   **End of Round:** A round ends when any player successfully plays their last card.\n\n**State Tracking:**\n*   `current_player_id`: (0 for human, 1-N for AI).\n*   `game_direction`: `enum` (CLOCKWISE, COUNTER_CLOCKWISE).\n*   `player_hands`: An array of arrays, each sub-array holding `Card` objects for a player's hand.\n*   `draw_pile`: An array of `Card` objects (the deck).\n*   `discard_pile`: An array of `Card` objects (the played cards).\n*   `current_color`: `enum` (RED, GREEN, BLUE, YELLOW), indicates the required color to match.\n*   `last_played_card`: `Card` object, representing the top card on the discard pile.\n*   `game_state`: `enum` (MAIN_MENU, LEVEL_INTRO, PLAYING, PAUSED, ROUND_WIN, ROUND_LOSE, GAME_COMPLETE, HIGH_SCORES).\n*   `current_score`: Integer, human player's score for the current game.\n*   `high_scores_list`: Array of objects `{name: string, score: integer}`.\n*   `current_level`: Integer, tracks current level number.",
  "visual_elements": "All elements will be rendered using p5.js drawing functions.\n\n*   **Cards:**\n    *   **Representation:** Rectangular shapes with rounded corners (e.g., `rect(x, y, w, h, radius)`). Dimensions: ~60x90 pixels.\n    *   **Colors:** Distinct background fills for Red (`#FF0000`), Green (`#00FF00`), Blue (`#0000FF`), Yellow (`#FFFF00`). White border (2px stroke) for all cards.\n    *   **Numbers (0-9):** Large white text (e.g., Arial bold, 48pt) centered on the card.\n    *   **Action Cards (Skip, Reverse, Draw Two):** White symbols centered on the card, accompanied by small text below (e.g., 'X' for Skip, '\u21bb' for Reverse, '+2' for Draw Two). Text in 18pt white.\n    *   **Wild Cards (Wild, Wild Draw Four):** Multi-colored background (e.g., four quadrants of Red, Green, Blue, Yellow). 'WILD' text centered (48pt white). 'WILD DRAW FOUR' also includes '+4' text below 'WILD'.\n    *   **Card Back:** Generic design (e.g., game logo or stylized pattern) with a consistent background color (e.g., dark blue).\n\n*   **Player Hands:**\n    *   **Human Player's Hand:** Cards displayed fanned out in an arc at the bottom of the screen. Individual cards are interactive (clickable). Hovering over a card makes it slightly larger or changes its border color.\n    *   **AI Player Hands:** Represented by stacks of card backs for each AI, positioned around the screen (e.g., top-center for AI1, left-center for AI2, right-center for AI3). A number displayed on each stack indicates the card count for that AI.\n\n*   **Draw Pile:** A stack of card backs, positioned to the left of the Discard Pile. Clickable to draw a card.\n\n*   **Discard Pile:** A stack of cards, centrally located, with only the top card fully visible. This top card determines the current playable color/number.\n\n*   **UI Elements:**\n    *   **Current Player Indicator:** A highlight (e.g., glowing border or small arrow) around the active player's hand/card stack.\n    *   **Current Color Indicator:** A small, filled circle or square (e.g., 30x30px) displaying the `current_color`, positioned near the Discard Pile.\n    *   **Action Prompts:** Text overlays for player actions (e.g., 'Choose a color', 'Draw 2 cards', 'Your Turn').\n    *   **Score Display:** Top-right corner of the screen: \"SCORE: [Current Score]\" (24pt white text).\n    *   **Level Display:** Top-left corner of the screen: \"LEVEL: [Current Level]\" (24pt white text).\n    *   **UNO Button/Prompt:** For levels requiring manual UNO call, a small clickable button or text prompt \"Press 'Z' for UNO!\" appears when player has one card left.\n    *   **Menu/Game Over Screens:** Centralized text for titles, dynamic buttons (e.g., 'Start Game', 'Next Level', 'Retry', 'Main Menu') with hover effects.\n    *   **High Score Table:** Tabular display of names and scores.\n\n*   **Animations & Visual Effects:**\n    *   **Card Movement:** Smooth interpolation (lerp) of position and rotation when cards are played, drawn, or dealt.\n    *   **Card Highlight:** Subtle glow or border change on selected/hovered cards.\n    *   **Card Shuffle:** Brief visual effect on the Draw Pile (e.g., cards briefly scatter and re-stack) when the discard pile is shuffled to form a new draw pile.\n    *   **UNO Call Effect:** A brief screen flash or particle effect with accompanying sound for a successful UNO call.\n    *   **Turn Indicator:** A subtle pulsing glow around the current player's area.",
  "control_scheme": "The primary control scheme will be mouse/touch-based, with keyboard shortcuts for common actions.\n\n*   **Mouse Left-Click / Touch Tap:**\n    *   **On a card in player's hand:** Attempt to play the selected card. If the card is playable, it animates to the discard pile. If not, a 'deny' sound plays.\n    *   **On the Draw Pile:** Draw a card from the draw pile and add it to the player's hand.\n    *   **On Color Choice Buttons:** Selects the new active color after playing a Wild card.\n    *   **On UI Buttons:** Activates menu options ('Start Game', 'Next Level', 'Retry', 'Main Menu', 'Resume', etc.).\n\n*   **Keyboard Controls:**\n    *   `ArrowLeft`: Select (highlight) the previous card in the human player's hand. Cycling through the hand.\n    *   `ArrowRight`: Select (highlight) the next card in the human player's hand. Cycling through the hand.\n    *   `Space`: Plays the currently highlighted card in hand. If no card is highlighted or the highlighted card is not playable, and the player has no other playable cards, this action defaults to drawing a card from the Draw Pile.\n    *   `Z`: (Active for Level 4 and 5 only) Declares 'UNO' when the human player has exactly one card left in their hand. This must be pressed after playing a card and before the next player's turn fully begins.",
  "game_states": "**MAIN_MENU:**\n*   **Description:** The initial screen displaying game title, 'Start Game', 'How to Play', and 'High Scores' buttons.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Start Game' -> `LEVEL_INTRO` (for Level 1).\n    *   Click 'High Scores' -> `HIGH_SCORES`.\n\n**LEVEL_INTRO:**\n*   **Description:** Displays the current level number, level title, and any special rules or changes for the upcoming level. A brief pause (e.g., 3 seconds) or 'Continue' button.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   After timeout or 'Continue' button click -> `PLAYING`.\n\n**PLAYING:**\n*   **Description:** The main game loop where players take turns playing cards. Includes rendering of hands, piles, current color, score, and level.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Human player plays last card -> `ROUND_WIN`.\n    *   AI player plays last card -> `ROUND_LOSE`.\n    *   Player presses 'P' (or dedicated pause button) -> `PAUSED`.\n\n**PAUSED:**\n*   **Description:** Game execution is halted. Displays 'Game Paused', 'Resume', and 'Main Menu' options.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Resume' -> `PLAYING`.\n    *   Click 'Main Menu' -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n\n**ROUND_WIN:**\n*   **Description:** Displays \"Round Won!\" along with the points earned for the round and total score. Options to 'Next Level' (if not final level) or 'Main Menu'.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Next Level' (if `current_level < MAX_LEVELS`) -> `LEVEL_INTRO` (for `current_level + 1`).\n    *   Click 'Next Level' (if `current_level == MAX_LEVELS`) -> `GAME_COMPLETE`.\n    *   Click 'Main Menu' -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n\n**ROUND_LOSE:**\n*   **Description:** Displays \"Round Lost!\" and the final score for the game (0 points awarded for the round). Options to 'Retry Level' or 'Main Menu'.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Retry Level' -> `LEVEL_INTRO` (for the `current_level`).\n    *   Click 'Main Menu' -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n\n**GAME_COMPLETE:**\n*   **Description:** Displays \"Congratulations! You completed all levels!\" and the final total score. Prompts player to enter a name for the high score list. After entry, shows 'Main Menu' button.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Main Menu' -> `MAIN_MENU`.\n\n**HIGH_SCORES:**\n*   **Description:** Displays a list of top scores with player names. 'Back' button to return to main menu.\n*   **Transitions:**\n    *   Click 'Back' -> `MAIN_MENU`.",
  "win_conditions": "**Round Win:**\n*   The human player successfully plays their last card, emptying their hand.\n\n**Game Win (Overall):**\n*   The human player successfully wins all 5 defined levels. Upon winning Level 5, the `GAME_COMPLETE` state is triggered.\n\n**Score System:** While not a win condition, the accumulated score across rounds is crucial for high score tracking and defines success beyond simply winning rounds.",
  "lose_conditions": "**Round Lose:**\n*   An AI opponent successfully plays their last card, emptying their hand before the human player.\n\n**Game Lose (Overall):**\n*   The human player fails to win a round (i.e., an AI wins the round) and chooses not to 'Retry Level', instead returning to the `MAIN_MENU`.\n*   The human player fails the final level (Level 5) and chooses not to 'Retry Level', returning to the `MAIN_MENU`.",
  "scoring_system": "A comprehensive scoring system is implemented to reward strategic play and provide replayability through high scores.\n\n**Points awarded per round (if human player wins):**\n*   Points are calculated from all cards remaining in AI opponents' hands:\n    *   **Numbered Cards (0-9):** Face value (e.g., a '7' card is worth 7 points).\n    *   **Action Cards (Skip, Reverse, Draw Two):** 20 points each.\n    *   **Wild Cards (Wild, Wild Draw Four):** 50 points each.\n*   The sum of these points is added to the human player's `current_score`.\n\n**Bonus Points (for human player winning a round):**\n*   **Speed Bonus:** +50 points if the round is won within a predefined time limit (e.g., 2 minutes).\n*   **Flawless Victory:** +100 points if the player wins the round without ever drawing a card from the Draw Pile.\n*   **Strategic Finish:** +25 points if the player's last card played was a Wild Draw Four.\n\n**Round Lose:**\n*   If an AI opponent wins a round, the human player earns 0 points for that round.\n\n**High Score System:**\n*   The `GAME_COMPLETE` state prompts the player to enter a name. The `current_score` (total accumulated across all won rounds) is then saved along with the name to a `high_scores_list`. This list stores the top 5 scores, sorted descending.\n\n**Score Display:**\n*   **Position:** The current accumulated score will be prominently displayed in the **top-right corner** of the playing screen during the `PLAYING` state.\n*   **Format:** `text(\"SCORE: \" + currentScore, width - 150, 30);` (using p5.js, example coordinates) ensuring clear readability with a contrasting color.\n\n**Score Affects Gameplay:**\n*   The score does not directly affect in-round gameplay (e.g., card probabilities or AI behavior). Its primary function is to serve as a metric for player performance, drive replayability, and establish rankings in the high score system.",
  "level_system": "The game features 5 progressively challenging levels, each introducing new complexities or AI behaviors.\n\n**Level 1: \"Friendly Intro\"**\n*   **Difficulty:** Easy.\n*   **Changes:** 3 AI opponents. AI opponents play cards strategically but do not aggressively target the human player with action cards. The human player's 'UNO' call is automatic (no penalty for forgetting).\n*   **Advancement:** Win the round -> Level 2.\n*   **Display:** Level intro screen: \"Level 1: Friendly Intro - Learn the Ropes! Your UNO calls are automatic.\"\n\n**Level 2: \"Strategic Opponents\"**\n*   **Difficulty:** Medium.\n*   **Changes:** 3 AI opponents. AI now prioritizes playing action cards more strategically (e.g., a Skip card if the next player is low on cards, or a Reverse card to avoid a specific player). AI will target the human player with Draw Two/Wild Draw Four cards more often if tactically advantageous. Human player's UNO call remains automatic.\n*   **Advancement:** Win the round -> Level 3.\n*   **Display:** Level intro screen: \"Level 2: Strategic Opponents - Watch Your Back! AI is getting smarter.\"\n\n**Level 3: \"Aggressive Play\"**\n*   **Difficulty:** Hard.\n*   **Changes:** 3 AI opponents. AI aggressively uses Wild Draw Four cards, even when they might have a matching color, to force the human player to draw more cards. AI focuses on playing cards as fast as possible to win. Human player's UNO call remains automatic, but a visual reminder that it will change for next levels is subtly introduced.\n*   **Advancement:** Win the round -> Level 4.\n*   **Display:** Level intro screen: \"Level 3: Aggressive Play - No Mercy! Get ready for manual UNO next level.\"\n\n**Level 4: \"Master Challenger\"**\n*   **Difficulty:** Very Hard.\n*   **Changes:** Now features **4 AI opponents** (total 5 players in the game). The human player **must manually call 'UNO'** by pressing the 'Z' key when down to 1 card. Failure to do so before the next player's turn results in drawing 2 penalty cards. AI becomes very adept at playing 'finishing' cards.\n*   **Advancement:** Win the round -> Level 5.\n*   **Display:** Level intro screen: \"Level 4: Master Challenger - Prove Your Mettle! (Don't Forget to Press 'Z' for UNO!)\"\n\n**Level 5: \"Ultimate Showdown\"**\n*   **Difficulty:** Extreme.\n*   **Changes:** 4 AI opponents. The human player starts with only 5 cards, while AI players still start with 7. AI opponents are given a slight initial advantage (e.g., their starting hand is guaranteed to contain at least 2 action cards). AI plays optimally and ruthlessly targets the human player with all available action cards.\n*   **Advancement:** Win the round -> `GAME_COMPLETE` state.\n*   **Display:** Level intro screen: \"Level 5: Ultimate Showdown - Grand Finale! Face the ultimate challenge.\"\n\n**Level Indicator Display:**\n*   During `PLAYING` state, the current level will be displayed in the **top-left corner** of the screen: `text(\"LEVEL: \" + currentLevel, 50, 30);` (using p5.js, example coordinates) with clear visibility.",
  "simplifications": "*   **Multiplayer Functionality:** The original \"\u4e00\u8d77\u4f18\u8bfa\" implies online multiplayer. This implementation will be strictly single-player, with all opponents controlled by AI.\n*   **Real-time Interactions:** Features like in-game chat, emotes, or direct player-to-player interaction are removed.\n*   **Customization:** Player avatars, custom card backs, and other cosmetic customizations are excluded to focus on core gameplay.\n*   **Monetization/Meta-game:** No in-app purchases, daily quests, or extensive progression systems beyond levels and high scores.\n*   **'Challenge Wild Draw 4' Rule:** The rule allowing players to challenge a Wild Draw 4 card if they suspect the player had a matching color is simplified. AI will not challenge a human player's Wild Draw 4. The human player cannot challenge an AI's Wild Draw 4.\n*   **'UNO' Call Mechanics:** For early levels (1-3), the human player's UNO call is automated to reduce complexity. For later levels (4-5), it becomes a manual action (pressing 'Z') as a difficulty increase. AI players will always correctly and automatically call 'UNO'.\n*   **Complex AI Personalities:** AI opponents will follow a general strategy that scales with difficulty, rather than having distinct personalities or complex learning algorithms.\n*   **Sound Effects:** Simplified to essential game events (card play, draw, win/lose, UNO call), rather than a full suite of ambient sounds and music."
}
</task>

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }

Color Match Royale

Welcome to Color Match Royale! Race against AI opponents to be the first to empty your hand of cards. Match the color or number of the top card on the discard pile. Use action cards strategically to skip opponents, reverse turn order, or force them to draw cards. Wild cards let you change the active color. Progress through 5 increasingly challenging levels, from friendly beginners to ruthless experts. Score points by winning rounds - the more cards left in opponents' hands, the higher your score!

Arrow Left/Right: Navigate through your hand to select a card | Space: Play the selected card (or draw if no valid play) | Z: Call "UNO" when you have one card left (Levels 4-5 only) | Enter: Start game from main menu | Esc: Pause/unpause during gameplay | R: Return to main menu from game over screen
